insights: HR holds only 5.6% of all assets but accounts for 12.81% of total asset costs, indicating severe cost concentration disproportionate to its asset volume.

HR has the highest average asset cost ($4,874.25), which is 2.07x higher than Finance (the next highest at $2,352.70), driven entirely by elevated exposure to high-cost categories—not higher volume or broader category diversity.

HR owns 66.7% of all Servers and 71.4% of all Web Servers in the organization—despite these two categories representing just 9 of HR’s 28 assets—making them the dominant cost drivers behind HR’s outlier average cost.

Servers ($8,524 avg) and Web Servers ($8,000 avg) are the two most expensive asset categories in the organization, and HR’s near-monopoly ownership of them explains its cost imbalance more than any other factor.

Customer Support and Sales collectively hold 65.8% of all assets, revealing a structural concentration of asset volume outside HR—yet HR remains the sole department with extreme cost-per-asset distortion, signaling a unique governance or provisioning issue rather than systemic over-provisioning.

HR’s Computer assets (19 units, $3,222 avg) align closely with peer departments’ computer costs, confirming that its cost anomaly is isolated to Server and Web Server holdings—not general IT equipment.
summary: The HR department exhibits a pronounced cost-efficiency gap: although it holds only 5.6% of organizational assets—well below the uniform expectation of 14.29%—it bears 12.81% of total asset costs and records the highest average asset cost by a wide margin ($4,874.25). This imbalance is not due to quantity or broad category sprawl, but stems almost exclusively from HR’s disproportionate ownership of the two most expensive asset types—Servers (66.7% of org total) and Web Servers (71.4% of org total)—which inflate its average cost despite comprising only 32% of its asset count. In contrast, its Computer assets reflect typical cost levels, confirming the anomaly is category-specific and operationally isolatable. Given this clear root cause, the immediate focus for optimization should be reviewing the business justification, utilization rates, and lifecycle status of HR’s Server and Web Server assets to determine whether consolidation, reallocation, or refresh timing adjustments can reduce cost concentration without compromising function.
